items.q: What is the worst-case complexity of bubble sort?
items.misconception: O(n) because it just passes through the list.
items.why: Students count one pass and forget that bubble sort does up to n passes, each comparing up to n elements. The nested loop structure gives n times n = n squared.

items.q: Which item is removed first from a stack?
items.why: Students confuse stacks (LIFO - like a stack of plates) with queues (FIFO - like a checkout line). The 'stack of plates' analogy makes LIFO concrete.

items.correct: The ability of different classes to respond to the same method call in their own way.
items.misconception: Having multiple classes with the same name.
items.random_wrong: Variables that change type at runtime.
items.why: Students confuse polymorphism with naming. Polymorphism means 'many forms' - a single interface (method signature) behaving differently depending on the object's actual class.

items.q: Which technique involves breaking a problem into smaller sub-problems?
items.why: It's Decomposition. “Abstraction” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: What is the purpose of a trace table?
items.correct: To track variable values during execution
items.why: The answer examiners want is: To track variable values during execution. “To compile code” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many distinct shapes are commonly used in basic flowcharts (start/end, process, decision, input/output)?
items.why: Exact answer: 4 shapes. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
